segments.title: Fairmile D Dog Boats in Britain's coastal fleet
segments.summary: An introduction to the Fairmile D Dog Boat and the Fairmile yard's importance to the Royal Navy's wartime coastal fleet.
segments.body: Dr. Clarke introduces the Fairmile D motor gun boat, known as the Dog Boat, as a particularly important type among Britain's small coastal combatants. He also recommends Leonard C. Reynolds's book on the craft, identifying a useful route into their history. Fairmile's rapid production of motor torpedo boats, motor gun boats, and motor launches supplied the Royal Navy with much of its World War II coastal fleet. The more familiar Fairmile B and its many launch and gun-boat variants provide the family comparison that makes the D's distinctive scale stand out.

evidence.note: The transcript introduces the Fairmile D and describes Fairmile's output of coastal craft for the Royal Navy.

segments.title: A 109-ton coastal fighting craft
segments.summary: The Fairmile D's 109-ton displacement and heavier armament distinguish it from the smaller craft usually associated with coastal forces.
segments.body: The Dog Boat is presented as the large and powerful member of the Fairmile family rather than another small launch. Dr. Clarke gives its displacement as 109 tons, making it heavier than an E-boat. He also stresses that the D carried substantially more firepower than that German comparison. Together, weight and armament explain why this coastal craft could perform a more forceful motor-gun-boat role than its modest dimensions might suggest.

evidence.note: The transcript calls the D the big, powerful boat and gives its 109-ton displacement, E-boat comparison, and firepower.

segments.title: Guns, torpedoes, and D-Day coverage
segments.summary: Some Dog Boats exchanged part of their gun armament for torpedoes, yet retained enough firepower for the coastal-force tasks needed around D-Day.
segments.body: Later in the war, the changing demand for motor torpedo boats led to some Fairmile Ds receiving torpedoes in place of part of their gun battery. Dr. Clarke links that demand directly to coastal-force coverage for D-Day. The conversions did not turn the boats into lightly armed craft, because they retained substantial gun power for engagements that still required it. The resulting mixed fit shows how the D could be adapted between motor-torpedo-boat and motor-gun-boat needs without surrendering its defining firepower.

evidence.note: The transcript describes replacing some guns with torpedoes while retaining firepower for D-Day coastal-force duties.

segments.title: Why not a modern Dog Boat?
segments.summary: A closing comparison asks why calls for a modern Flower-class corvette are not matched by calls for a modern Fairmile D Dog Boat.
segments.body: Dr. Clarke ends with a force-design question rather than a simple historical comparison. When people advocate a modern Flower-class corvette, he asks why they do not instead call for a modern Fairmile D. The contrast puts a small, heavily armed coastal combatant beside the more familiar corvette reference point. It invites viewers to think about which wartime capabilities, not merely which famous hull types, a modern force might actually need.

evidence.note: The transcript contrasts calls for a modern Flower-class corvette with the idea of a modern Fairmile D Dog Boat.
